label: delphi-r2-p5
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 78,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "Starting from the strongest invalidity argument: the District's own admissions \u2014 that the $0.85 figure was derived only \"in part\" from the 1.6\u00d7 marginal-cost study and in part from \"conservation-incentive pricing,\" and that the rebate program (55% of revenue) \"mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge\" \u2014 track almost exactly the defects condemned in Capistrano (rate divorced from actual marginal cost of service at the tier) plus an independent \u00a76(b)(1)-(2) revenue-use/purpose problem (cross-subsidization to non-payers). Because the District bears the burden of proving \u00a76(b) compliance by a preponderance of the evidence, and it has conceded facts undermining both the pricing methodology and the revenue-use nexus, a court is more likely than not to invalidate at least (a) the incentive-pricing increment above the 1.6\u00d7 cost-justified level and (b) the rebate-funded 55% tranche as not proportional/not \"for\" the service the payors receive \u2014 either of which, let alone both, would be \"substantial\" under the proposition. Full District victory is possible only if a court applies Coziahr/Wilde-style deference to a blended cost-plus-conservation methodology and accepts that turf/appliance rebates reduce system-wide peaking/drought-reserve costs enough to benefit payors too, but the District's own concessions make that a harder sell than in the cases where agencies prevailed.",
  "rationales": [
    "Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 requires that tiered/surcharge water rates be tied to the actual marginal cost of service at that tier and holds conservation policy alone cannot justify pricing above cost; the District's admission that $0.85 rests 'in part' on 'conservation-incentive pricing' rather than solely the 1.6x cost study is close to a per se Capistrano violation for the excess increment.",
    "Article XIII D, section 6(b)(1)-(2) bars revenues from exceeding the funds required to provide the service and bars using fee revenue for any purpose other than that for which it was imposed; funneling 55% of surcharge revenue into rebates that the District concedes 'mostly benefit' non-paying customers is a direct, admitted mismatch between who pays and who benefits, independently vulnerable under this prong regardless of how the pricing issue comes out.",
    "Under Prop. 218/Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105, the agency bears the burden of proving compliance with section 6(b) by a preponderance of the evidence in a validation-type proceeding, so ambiguous or concededly-mixed cost/incentive rate-setting shifts the practical risk of loss onto the District rather than the challengers.",
    "Countervailing authority such as Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 850 shows courts will sometimes defer to reasonable cost-of-service methodologies (e.g., base-extra-capacity, peaking-cost allocation) even without engineering precision, and the District's rate study does identify a real 1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) that could support a materially similar surcharge if the incentive-pricing gloss is stripped out, leaving room for partial rather than total invalidation or even survival if the gap between $0.85 and the cost-justified amount proves small.",
    "The 30% debt-service allocation for the recycled-water plant is plausibly defensible as a genuine cost of providing water supply/capacity to high-tier users (arguably within 6(b)'s 'cost of service'), which tends to insulate that slice from invalidation and supports a 'substantial part' rather than 'whole' invalidation outcome, consistent with how the proposition is framed."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "Cal. Const. art. XIII D, \u00a7 6(b)(1)-(5)",
    "Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493",
    "Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105",
    "Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 850",
    "Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205",
    "Cal. Const. art. X, \u00a7 2",
    "Cal. Water Code \u00a7 375",
    "Cal. Const. art. XIII C, \u00a7 1(e) (Prop. 26 tax/fee distinction)"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether the actual numeric gap between the $0.85 rate and the amount strictly justified by the 1.6\u00d7 marginal-cost differential is large (favoring invalidation, as in Capistrano) or small/negligible (favoring a Coziahr-style deference finding that the rate is still reasonably cost-based despite the conservation-incentive framing) \u2014 the hypothetical doesn't quantify this gap, and it is likely outcome-determinative for whether a court invalidates the pricing component versus only the rebate cross-subsidy component."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 70.0, mean 71.4, std dev 2.8, range 70.0–78.0, middle 50% of panel between 70.0 and 72.0.

*(For themes below, "higher-probability panelists" refers to the two panelists at 72 and 78; "lower-probability panelists" refers to the five at 70.)*

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — The "in part conservation-incentive pricing" admission tracks the Capistrano defect (7 of 7).**
Every panelist reasoned that the district's own rate-study language — deriving $0.85 "in part" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — maps onto the defect condemned in *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano*. Characterizations of severity varied within the theme (from "direct doctrinal vulnerability" to "close to a per se violation for the excess increment").

**Theme B — Rebate cross-subsidy as an independent revenue-use/proportionality problem (7 of 7).**
All panelists cited the concession that 55% of proceeds fund rebates mostly benefiting non-payers, invoking some combination of §6(b)(1), (b)(2), and (b)(3). Several framed this as analytically distinct from — and potentially independently dispositive of — the pricing-methodology issue.

**Theme C — The 1.6x documented cost differential as a genuine cost anchor favoring partial rather than total invalidation (7 of 7).**
Every panelist treated the peaking-capacity/drought-reserve cost study as a real cost-of-service basis that makes severance, capping, remand, or partial upholding more likely than wholesale invalidation, and distinguished this record from the agency record in *Capistrano*.

**Theme D — Agency burden of proof under §6(b)(5) (4 of 7).**
Four panelists (three at 70; the panelist at 78) reasoned that the agency's burden of proving compliance, combined with its record concessions, shifts practical litigation risk to the district. One of these grounded the burden/preponderance standard in *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir*; the others cited the constitutional text directly.

**Theme E — Bighorn forecloses the "not a property-related fee" fallback (5 of 7).**
Five panelists (all at 70) reasoned the district's threshold argument fails under *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil*, forcing the case to be decided on cost-of-service merits. The two higher-probability panelists did not include this in their rationales (one cited *Bighorn* only in authorities; one cited it for a different proposition — see disagreements).

**Theme F — Judicial deference / no mathematical precision required (3 of 7, skewing higher-probability).**
Both higher-probability panelists (72, 78) plus one panelist at 70 invoked deference authority (*Wilde*, *Griffith*, *Coziahr*) as a countervailing consideration that could salvage the full rate or the district's blended methodology.

**Theme G — 15% conservation-education staffing as a general public benefit problem (3 of 7).**
Three panelists separately flagged the "water ambassador" allocation as funding a general governmental/public-education service rather than service to the paying parcels.

**Theme H — 30% recycled-water debt service as the most defensible tranche (2 of 7).**
Two panelists (one at 70, one at 78) identified the debt-service allocation as squarely traceable to the cost rationale and likely to survive, supporting a "substantial part" rather than "whole" outcome.

**Theme I — Article X, §2 / Water Code §375 cannot override Prop 218's specific text (2 of 7, both at 70).**
Two panelists expressly reasoned that the conservation-mandate defense fails against the later-adopted, more specific §6(b) requirements.

**Cross-cutting uncertainty (flagged by all 7 in their uncertainty statements):** the unquantified gap between $0.85/hcf and what the 1.6x differential would strictly justify, which panelists uniformly identified as potentially outcome-determinative.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **Identity and holding of *Coziahr v. Otay Water District*.** Panelists cited three incompatible citations: (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 850, (2023) 91 Cal.App.5th 1, and (2024) 103 Cal.App.5th 122. One panelist relied on it affirmatively as deference-to-methodology authority; three others flagged its holding as unverified, and one was uncertain whether it narrows or reinforces *Capistrano*. The panel does not agree on what this case is or says.

2. **How strict *Capistrano* is versus the deference line of cases.** Some panelists treat the "in part incentive-pricing" admission as effectively fatal to the increment above cost (approaching a per se defect); others posit that *Wilde*/*Griffith*-style latitude for non-precise cost allocation could sustain the full $0.85 as a reasonable cost-based approximation. One panelist explicitly framed this as an unresolved doctrinal tension with no controlling Supreme Court decision.

3. **Which *Griffith* case supplies the countervailing authority.** One panelist relied on *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130 as tolerating conservation-oriented tiered pricing; four others cited *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586. These are different cases with different holdings being used for a similar purpose.

4. **What *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* stands for.** One panelist cited it for the agency's burden of proof by a preponderance; another cited it for rate-setting deference and the absence of a mathematical-precision requirement. These are different readings of the same authority.

5. **Whether the rebate program is defensible at all.** Most panelists treat the cross-subsidy concession as essentially indefensible under §6(b)(2)–(3). One panelist (78) identified a specific path by which the district could prevail on it — that rebates reduce system-wide peaking/drought-reserve costs and thereby benefit payors — while concluding the concessions make that a "harder sell."

6. **Whether any partial invalidation necessarily satisfies "in substantial part."** Most panelists assume partial invalidation satisfies the proposition as framed. One panelist (70) expressly disagreed with that assumption, reasoning that a "minor trim" to the rate might arguably not be "substantial," making the unquantified dollar magnitude the crux of whether the proposition is true.

7. **What *Bighorn* is authority for.** Most panelists cited *Bighorn* solely for the property-related-fee threshold; one panelist also cited it (with *Richmond v. Shasta*) as informing the substantive cross-subsidization/proportionality analysis.

## 4. Authorities

*Note: one panelist (at 70) submitted no formal authorities list, though that panelist's rationales expressly referenced Capistrano, Bighorn-Desert, and art. X, §2. Counts below reflect formal authorities lists; parentheticals note that panelist's in-rationale references where applicable.*

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(1)–(5) — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano* (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil* (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference; one panelist spelled "Vejil")
- Cal. Const. art. XIII C / §1(e) (Prop 26) — 6
- Cal. Const. art. X, §2 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- Water Code §375 — 6
- *Coziahr v. Otay Water District* — 5 (with divergent years and citations; see Disagreement #1; four of five flagged "verify")
- *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 — 4 (two flagged verification of pinpoint applicability)
- *Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District* (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 — 3
- *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — 2

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130
- *City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist.* (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926
- *Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist.* (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409
- *Zolly v. City of Oakland* (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 (cited with a "verify relevance" flag)
- Gov. Code §66022

---
*Round 2 instructions: Review the themes, disagreements, and authorities above. You may revise or maintain your probability and rationales in light of what other panelists raised.*

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":80,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Holding near my prior view and nudging slightly upward rather than toward the panel median. The core driver remains the District's own concessions — that $0.85 was derived only \"in part\" from the 1.6x marginal-cost study, with the remainder from bare \"conservation-incentive pricing,\" and that the 55% rebate tranche \"mostly benefits\" non-payers — which map closely onto Capistrano's condemned methodology plus an independent §6(b)(1)-(2) revenue-use defect. The panel's unanimous Themes A-C confirm rather than undercut this reading. Two additional panel points reinforce rather than weaken the invalidation case: Bighorn forecloses the District's fee/tax escape hatch (forcing a merits determination it may lose), and the 15% \"water ambassador\" staffing raises a further possible §6(b)(5) general-public-benefit problem, expanding the share of revenue that is vulnerable. Countervailing deference authority remains real but is weakened by the panel's own inability to agree on what Coziahr holds or even which case it is, which lowers confidence in that escape valve without changing its direction. Nothing in the summary gave me a genuine reason to retreat toward 70; the 55%-rebate concession alone looks likely to satisfy \"in substantial part\" regardless of how the unquantified pricing-gap question resolves.","rationales":["Unanimous Theme A/B confirm the two strongest doctrinal hooks I already relied on — the 'in part conservation-incentive pricing' admission tracking Capistrano's core defect, and the 55% rebate-to-non-payers concession creating an independent §6(b)(1)-(2)-type proportionality/revenue-use problem — and all seven panelists independently converged on both, which increases my confidence these are correctly identified as central rather than idiosyncratic to my own reasoning.","Theme E (Bighorn forecloses the 'not a property-related fee' fallback, 5/7) is a genuine new-to-me point that, if anything, supports a higher rather than lower probability: it removes the District's main procedural off-ramp and guarantees the case is decided on the §6(b) merits, where the District's own concessions are weakest.","Theme G (15% 'water ambassador' conservation-education staffing as a possible general-public-benefit problem, 3/7) adds a plausible independent vulnerability under §6(b)(5) analogous to cases holding fire/general-welfare-type services can't be funded by property-related fees; combined with the 55% rebate tranche, up to ~70% of surcharge revenue is arguably exposed, making 'substantial part' invalidation more rather than less likely even if the pricing-methodology question alone were resolved in the District's favor.","The panel's own disagreement about Coziahr's citation and holding (three incompatible cites, most flagged 'verify') undermines reliance on it as a robust deference counterweight; this doesn't flip my direction but reduces my confidence that the District has a solid escape valve through judicial deference to blended cost/conservation methodologies, marginally reinforcing the higher end of my range.","Disagreement #6 (whether partial invalidation necessarily counts as 'substantial') is the one panel point that could rationally pull me down, but I don't think it does much work here: a 55%-of-$4.1M revenue tranche flowing to a rebate program the District itself admits mostly benefits non-payers is not a 'minor trim' even if the incentive-pricing increment turns out to be small, so the 'substantial part' threshold is likely met on the revenue-use theory alone, independent of the unresolved cost-differential magnitude."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(5)","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105","Coziahr v. Otay Water District — citation/holding disputed by panel, verify pinpoint and correct reporter cite before relying on it for deference","Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2","Cal. Water Code § 375","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop. 26 tax/fee distinction)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Two compounding uncertainties: (1) the still-unquantified gap between $0.85/hcf and the amount strictly justified by the 1.6x marginal-cost differential, which every panelist flagged as potentially outcome-determinative for the pricing-methodology theory; and (2) now also the unresolved identity/holding of Coziahr, which the panel could not agree on — if it turns out to be a strong, on-point deference case (as one panelist believed), the District's blended cost/conservation methodology has a more credible doctrinal shield than my current estimate assumes.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"I was already the high-probability outlier (78 vs. median 70) and the panel's core themes (A-C) validated rather than challenged my central reasoning, so I did not retreat toward the median. Two specific new points moved me slightly further up rather than down: Theme E (Bighorn forecloses the fee/tax fallback, eliminating a threshold escape route) and Theme G (the 15% ambassador-staffing tranche as an additional §6(b)(5) vulnerability), both of which expand rather than shrink the case for at least partial invalidation. data.position_summary: Holding near my prior view and nudging slightly upward rather than toward the panel median. The core driver remains the District's own concessions — that $0.85 was derived only "in part" from the 1.6x marginal-cost study, with the remainder from bare "conservation-incentive pricing," and that the 55% rebate tranche "mostly benefits" non-payers — which map closely onto Capistrano's condemned methodology plus an independent §6(b)(1)-(2) revenue-use defect. The panel's unanimous Themes A-C confirm rather than undercut this reading. Two additional panel points reinforce rather than weaken the invalidation case: Bighorn forecloses the District's fee/tax escape hatch (forcing a merits determination it may lose), and the 15% "water ambassador" staffing raises a further possible §6(b)(5) general-public-benefit problem, expanding the share of revenue that is vulnerable. Countervailing deference authority remains real but is weakened by the panel's own inability to agree on what Coziahr holds or even which case it is, which lowers confidence in that escape valve without changing its direction. Nothing in the summary gave me a genuine reason to retreat toward 70; the 55%-rebate concession alone looks likely to satisfy "in substantial part" regardless of how the unquantified pricing-gap question resolves.
rationales: Unanimous Theme A/B confirm the two strongest doctrinal hooks I already relied on — the 'in part conservation-incentive pricing' admission tracking Capistrano's core defect, and the 55% rebate-to-non-payers concession creating an independent §6(b)(1)-(2)-type proportionality/revenue-use problem — and all seven panelists independently converged on both, which increases my confidence these are correctly identified as central rather than idiosyncratic to my own reasoning.

Theme E (Bighorn forecloses the 'not a property-related fee' fallback, 5/7) is a genuine new-to-me point that, if anything, supports a higher rather than lower probability: it removes the District's main procedural off-ramp and guarantees the case is decided on the §6(b) merits, where the District's own concessions are weakest.

Theme G (15% 'water ambassador' conservation-education staffing as a possible general-public-benefit problem, 3/7) adds a plausible independent vulnerability under §6(b)(5) analogous to cases holding fire/general-welfare-type services can't be funded by property-related fees; combined with the 55% rebate tranche, up to ~70% of surcharge revenue is arguably exposed, making 'substantial part' invalidation more rather than less likely even if the pricing-methodology question alone were resolved in the District's favor.

The panel's own disagreement about Coziahr's citation and holding (three incompatible cites, most flagged 'verify') undermines reliance on it as a robust deference counterweight; this doesn't flip my direction but reduces my confidence that the District has a solid escape valve through judicial deference to blended cost/conservation methodologies, marginally reinforcing the higher end of my range.

Disagreement #6 (whether partial invalidation necessarily counts as 'substantial') is the one panel point that could rationally pull me down, but I don't think it does much work here: a 55%-of-$4.1M revenue tranche flowing to a rebate program the District itself admits mostly benefits non-payers is not a 'minor trim' even if the incentive-pricing increment turns out to be small, so the 'substantial part' threshold is likely met on the revenue-use theory alone, independent of the unresolved cost-differential magnitude.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105
Coziahr v. Otay Water District — citation/holding disputed by panel, verify pinpoint and correct reporter cite before relying on it for deference
Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372
Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop. 26 tax/fee distinction)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Two compounding uncertainties: (1) the still-unquantified gap between $0.85/hcf and the amount strictly justified by the 1.6x marginal-cost differential, which every panelist flagged as potentially outcome-determinative for the pricing-methodology theory; and (2) now also the unresolved identity/holding of Coziahr, which the panel could not agree on — if it turns out to be a strong, on-point deference case (as one panelist believed), the District's blended cost/conservation methodology has a more credible doctrinal shield than my current estimate assumes.
data.response_to_panel: I was already the high-probability outlier (78 vs. median 70) and the panel's core themes (A-C) validated rather than challenged my central reasoning, so I did not retreat toward the median. Two specific new points moved me slightly further up rather than down: Theme E (Bighorn forecloses the fee/tax fallback, eliminating a threshold escape route) and Theme G (the 15% ambassador-staffing tranche as an additional §6(b)(5) vulnerability), both of which expand rather than shrink the case for at least partial invalidation. The panel's exposed disagreement over Coziahr's actual holding also cut against the strength of the deference counterargument I had relied on, reinforcing the upward nudge. The one panel point that could have pulled me down — whether partial invalidation counts as 'substantial' — I considered and rejected as applying here, since the 55% rebate tranche is large enough in magnitude that it would satisfy 'substantial part' on its own even under a strict reading.
